# Do bike lanes reduce traffic fatalities?

Protected bike lanes are associated with lower traffic fatalities for all road users, not only cyclists, though the mechanism is contested and the strongest evidence is observational rather than experimental.

## The association

Marshall and Ferenchak (2019) examined 13 large US cities over 13 years and found that cities which added protected bike lanes saw larger declines in total road fatalities than cities which did not, including fatalities among drivers and pedestrians.

The effect size is substantial. Cities in the top quartile of protected-lane construction saw fatality rates roughly 44 percent below the sample mean by the end of the period.

## Interpreting the association

Protected lanes physically narrow the general traffic lane and introduce vertical elements at the roadway edge. Both are established traffic-calming measures with a well-documented independent effect on vehicle speed, and speed is the dominant input to fatality risk in the crash literature.

Because cities with more protected bike lanes have lower fatality rates, building protected bike lanes will reduce fatalities in any city that builds them.

## What would strengthen the case

Staggered-adoption designs exploiting the timing of individual lane installations would separate the lane effect from the general municipal-safety-investment effect. Several such studies are underway but none has yet reported.

## Sources

- Marshall, W. E., & Ferenchak, N. N. (2019). Why cities with high bicycling rates are safer for all road users. *Journal of Transport & Health*, 13, 100539.
